speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. September 20, 1995. Bluma Weinstock. You were talking about recovering from typhus and being transferred to a work section--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Auschwitz. Can you please tell me what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 1942. Yeah. And the-- I-- and I came to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] uh, they took me to work. I worked at [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was, um-- we have to make from plastic-- it was this long strip that said that need this for airplanes. And, uh, this was with tar, you know? It glued. And we have to-- we got the-- we got the little [NON-ENGLISH], we have to [INAUDIBLE] this, because we have to make so much. If you allowed those methods, uh, daily-- if you didn't make this that you know where you're going-- so we have to stay in-- we stayed and worked with-- it's hard to describe how-- was a little plastic, and we have to make very long methods. And, um, I worked there for a long time. Just one thing, and where we-- every day I did the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: The morning, this-- when I-- we went in the morning, uh, went to work. They wake us up in middle-- they wake us mid-- I said this already? Middle of the night. It Was dark. We have to-- maybe 5 o'clock. It's still dark. We have to go out and stay up hills, because they was counting us. [INAUDIBLE] light came. And then there was the light. We have to, uh, come the SS with the dogs. And we-- they took us to work. You-- you could not-- it could turn off. If not, the dog was jumping on you. You have to walk straight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think we walked probably-- they say, six, seven kilometers every day to the work. And in being at work, we always hear hollering, like-- and that's not far away from us was uh, they burned little children. They said they don't have enough gas for the children, and they burn-- they put them in the hills, and they burned them. And we hear all the crying from the little children. Every day. Every day was this. And the-- we-- food, we didn't got. We got a little bread in the morning. And we got the-- a coffee. And we-- and then we got a little margarine. And sometimes we got a little, uh, marmalade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they took away. Then we came to-- then they came from-- Szopienice to Auschwitz. They took away-- I got a ring from my mother, which I wear there. And I got a gold tooth. They took it out, too. They took it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any utensils to use for eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a red bowl. A-- a bowl. A red bowl. I lost that red bowl, and this you have to always save. If you wouldn't got this, you couldn't live. You can-- you cannot get your food. You have to sleep on that like a pillow. You hi-- in daytime you hang it up on your neck. You-- you went to work like that. And they all looked-- they shaved us. Every-- every two, three weeks, they shaved our hea-- we was happy that we didn't got no hair. Because we got a lot of lice. We-- we-- we was not-- you catch those lice from one person to the other person. My whole body was holes just from the lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked there for-- then they closed up that-- they said [INAUDIBLE] and they give me another work. They put me-- we went to work in water. Uh, they didn't need this work. They need just to torture us. And when I went-- we stayed in the water. And we have to work-- take off stones from the water. I don't know what-- what they needed. It was a German couple on the [INAUDIBLE], and she was watching us. And, uh, one time, one-- the couple came with a big stick. And she hurt my back. And I was think-- I-- I felt that I'd-- I'm already dead. Was there a reason that she hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said that I was staying and I didn't work, she said. And I said, I working the whole time. You couldn't-- you're not allowed to answer her, because she's-- they're hurting you. They're killing you. And every day, less people. Even without gas, people died. People died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends at this time? Anybody that you were close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just-- I got, uh, a woman. She was a little older, and she-- we-- we was laying on the same blankets. And she said-- I became malaria. I-- I got sick, and I became malaria. And she saw like I'm suffering. Because when the cold was first of all [INAUDIBLE]. And then my fever came out, and I felt better. And she started to feel so sorry on me. She said that she got a daughter or sister-- she looked like me. And she helped me. And, uh, she rubbed me. And she-- and she tried to do the best for me. I have to, in the morning, bring coffee in the big, uh, like, cans. She didn't let me. She did it for me. Because she saw that I am so sick and I have to go to work. And you have to pretend that you're not sick, because, you know, when you're coming back from work, the SS stayed, Mengele stayed-- they're all staying and watching you. Whether you're straight, or that you're not sick. If then, not, then they called you out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have selections?

speaker: interviewee
text: They make very often selections. Sometimes when they was busy with other people, they didn't select us. And when they was not so busy, they select us very often. They told us we should go in the wash rooms and they stated they'd select us. And they looked-- you have to get naked. And they said, it once was what I got, that they make you right and left, left and right. And I runned to the not good one. And he pulled me back, and he said to me, you know where did you run? You get so excited that you don't know where you're running. He pulled me back and he said, you will be tomorrow in the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know. I just always felt I'm saved. And I always felt that my mother holds me by my-- my hand. When I was in the-- every day and every minute, you are in danger. You don't know if you're going to live. But I always felt that she is always on me, and she is-- she saved-- I feel that she saved me. Where I was in back trouble, she was here for me. I've helped [INAUDIBLE]. In the night, when I was, um, I was sleeping, she always-- I wake up in the morning, I said to her, you know, I ate everything for the best you can get. I dream that my mother brought me everything to eat. And how I survived it, just by a miracle. I don't know how, really, I survived. So I worked in the water with the sh-- we couldn't take off the shoes. We have to wear all the time the shoes, because in the morning you couldn't put back. They were so wet and swollen. So then I-- we worked and still got really cold. You cannot work frozen. You cannot work in the water. Then they give me another job. Uh, stones. We have to schlep stones from one place to the other place. We have to-- they didn't need that work. Just to torture us, to show that we working. [SIGHS] And this was the jobs. And I got sick. And every day with the-- with the malaria. This was-- this was a torture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one time I get um-- my leg got swollen. My left, uh, leg got swollen, and I couldn't walk no more to work. And, um, you're afraid to say to somebody. Just, I fainted. And they took me on a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] is like a little hospital. And I was there. And then-- then came like a-- she was a Jewish couple. And she knows me from Sosnowiec. And she came in that hospital. And she saw me. She said to me, you know, if you're not going out from that tomorrow, they're coming and closing up everybody to the gas chamber. And I said, you see my leg. I couldn't walk. It's swollen. You know, the past is coming. And she said, I'm going to talk to-- to the block, to the-- from the-- from the hospital. She should let you out. It doesn't matter how you're going to feel. You have to go out. And she talked to her and she let me out. And she said that uh-- it was a nurse, a doctor-- she said I should come after work, and she's going to clean up my leg after this. You know, they operate my leg. It came a doctor from the men's, and they cut off and they took it out from my leg. And I remember the doctor said that I would wait a day, I probably would go, that to my heart I couldn't live. I was lucky that the man came. I couldn't understand myself. And I came out, and-- and I went. A day, two days, later, the whole-- the hospital, everybody went to the gas chamber. They cleaned up. And then you-- then you-- it's so much to tell. Then you was walking and looking in the sky. You could see just faces. Heads. Eyes. And always was smoke. And it was so-- it smelled from the bones. You could feel. And every time you could see. I remember when the [INAUDIBLE] transport came, and they put-- we went too. We walked to work. We could see. They all went right away to the ga-- the gas chamber. He was lucky-- some was lucky. They holed up. And then later, I was so sick I start to spit with blood. And I-- and I-- I didn't know what-- what to do. And I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the doctor. And she said, I'm sorry. I can do nothing for you. You have-- you-- you have TB. You have-- you're spitting from your lungs. And then I had-- I didn't know what to do, what I can do. I know I'm not going to live. And I-- when I see somebody die, I said, oh, he's so lucky. I have to go through what he has already. And what I remember, when we went to work, if somebody died working, we passed-- we stepped on them. If somebody lost a piece of bread, what they got, everybody was running to pick that piece of bread. And if somebody died, we stepped on them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one-- this was almost the end. They said they're going to close up, uh, our-- the barracks where we stayed. And they put us-- they said-- they said that we're going to come out from Auschwitz. We're going to go out from there. And we want-- we couldn't believe it. And that closed up, and they put us to the [INAUDIBLE]. They called it [NON-ENGLISH], the [INAUDIBLE]. It was there in those barracks. And they took us there, and they said from there we're going to go to another camp. Do you remember what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably '43, '44. Because I was two years in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, were you able to hear any outside news about how the war was progressing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We co-- we didn't hear nothing. We know that we're going to end up there. That's all. [SIGHS] And then we went to the-- the other camp, that it's going-- the [INAUDIBLE] was there. They all got killed. No [INAUDIBLE] was left. Then we got even [INAUDIBLE], like we got the Jews there. And we stayed there, and they said to us that we have to wait. They-- they'll come, and they'll go-- we'll go on trains. We're going to go to another camp. They didn't say where. And before we-- then-- then we went to, you know, to the washrooms, or they they took us. And one time we went to the washrooms in [INAUDIBLE], and they said, you know, you have to take off your clothes and not to take with you. You have to leave these clothes here. We know if they said to you, you go in the washrooms and you cannot take your-- the clothes, they're going to use the-- the gas on you, for the gas chamber. And we came there and she said, come the-- the Gestapo, you leave everything here. You get new clothes, they said. And it was one girl from [INAUDIBLE]. And she said to us, to the g-- [INAUDIBLE], we're here two years. You don't want to go to the gas chamber. One way they're going to gas us. You have-- we have to run. And they break up the windows. And we went out. And they start to-- with dogs, they start to run after us. And we said, you cannot kill. You cannot do anything you wanted, but we are not going to go to the gas chamber. After two years being here in Auschwitz, we're not going to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to the barracks. Some of them probably got killed. We don't know. And then we stayed there, probably, I don't know, maybe for two months. And they said, we're leaving from there. They're closing up. Must be that time, the end of Auschwitz already. Because we hear that there's some crematoriums got already destroyed. So they took us on-- on the-- those trains in the-- go in the trains, they said we're going to Bergen-Belsen. We didn't know what Bergen-Belsen is. We know it's a camp. And I think we went there-- maybe it was two days on the trains. And a lot of people died, for no food, for no drinks, for not nothing. And they throwing up from the-- from the trains. They throwed up outside, running the trains, who was dead. So we came to Bergen-Belsen. When I came to Bergen-Belsen, that girlfriend what she saw me in the [NON-ENGLISH] when I was in that hospital, when she-- what she told me, I have to go out. She came to me. She said, you know what here is. Here you don't have a gas chamber. Here they don't use gas. Here they put poison in the food. If you're going to eat that-- that food, you not gonna-- you'll live a few days, and you're going to die. And I said, what we can do different?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said to me, you know, I try to help you as much I can. You should not eat that soup. Because I know what they're doing here. She was like, um-- she's alive. She's-- she's in Florida. And she's-- she said, I going to try to-- to bring you something, a potato or the-- a bread, the something. You should not eat the soup. And that-- Bergen-Belsen-- was so bad. Auschwitz was bad. Bergen-Belsen was ju-- they-- they didn't get the crematorium, that's true. They didn't make no selections, that is true. Just people died like flies. They ate the soup. I remember, it came transport from French, from Paris, just beautiful girls. They even make shows for us. And day to day, they was laying dead. And she was very good to me. And I didn't eat that soup. And, uh, she brought me some-- what she could do. I probably was not the only one. She got probably other friends, too, which she helped us. And this-- she helped me in-- and I didn't eat. Just my job was-- not just mine-- a lot, but we have to pick up all of the dead people and put them in wagons. And we have to take them away. In-- in-- it was a special place, where we'd buried. And the-- and this was the job what we have to do every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably was 20, 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think you coped with these conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know myself. I don't know. This is why I said it's so painful. It's so painful to tell this everything. And beside this, I said, if I wouldn't be there, if somebody would tell me those stories, I wouldn't believe it either. It's hard to believe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's very hard to believe. Just, you know, every day you saw people dying, dying, dying. Not many left in Bergen-Belsen. The only thing was you don't-- you-- you was not so afraid. You know you're going to die. But you're not afraid the-- the selections, and the Mengele. And those-- and those-- to go through. From this you didn't have-- you wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. And, uh, I think this-- this was the life. We went through every day the same thing, the same thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got a friends there, what she was so close to me. She helped me a lot. We was working together. And I was still-- didn't feel good. I was spitting with blood. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, one time they start to talk, we saw airplanes running. That maybe they come and they put some bombs here. We all got killed. Not to suffer so much. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, uh, one time it-- they came-- this was the end, I think, of the war. It-- it came the end-- the [INAUDIBLE]. We was in the barracks, and it came out with trucks, the-- the-- the Army. We was thinking this is the Germans. They're going to take us away. And, um, it was one-- it was the English. And they start to-- to call us. We should come out. We should come out. We shouldn't be worried. You-- you free. You're not in the-- we-- we was afraid. So slowly, people start to go-- come out. It was-- one was a rabbi, a soldier from the-- from the English. And he started to say, you're safe. You don't have to be worried. And we was there for a while. Still they took us in-- in the camps, with-- the SS stayed there. We went to the other camps. And I think that time we was free. And this was not then. After a while, I got married. And I got sick and I have to go to a TB sanatorium for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be maybe '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened right after liberation? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation, we went to the-- to the camps, the Army camps. And we stayed there for a while. And, um-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there wa-- I was very sick. And-- and they said they're going to take me to Sweden. And the train was there, you know, standing for people to take to Sweden. So I said I'd go. They said people which they need sanitoriums, doctors, they want to take it. And then I went out and I said, when I go to Sweden, I will not find nobody. And I'm going to stay here in Bergen-Belsen, maybe I'll find somebody, a brother or somebody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I fo-- I-- we find some people which-- that survived. And they became our friends, like sisters. We lived together. Then, um, my husband-- he was not husband at that time-- he heard that I am alive. And he sent his brother to pick me up. Because he-- he already was busy. And I went to Schwandorf, Germany. Did you know your husband before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I know him before. And, uh, that time, I got a friend which, she saved me, too. She helped me. She was from French, but she said she-- her sister looked like me. I don't know. Then she came. She said she wanted to go and see who I'm going to marry. And she came with me-- she went with me to Schwandorf. And she saw my husband. And then she said, now I go back to French, look for my family. Maybe I find them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't went back. And I didn't want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out what happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we signed-- with camps in Germany, from one camp to the other, we signed the names. And we sent from one place to the other place. And so we find-- I found my two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed through '52. '52 we came to the United States. We came to-- when I was in the sanatorium and I came home, I registered-- we want to go to Israel. And Pincus got his brother in Israel. He said that I shouldn't come because Israel, they got food that time. I should better stay in Germany. And then they start to register to the United States. So I went with my husband to register. Him, let him-- they let right away. And to me he said I have-- with my lungs. And I didn't want to say that I was in a sanatorium.
